quick_facts.label: Professional scope
quick_facts.value: Physiotherapy involves examining and managing movement or functional problems, disability, healing and pain associated with trauma or disease through physical modalities such as exercise, mobilisation, manipulation and electro-physical agents; the national handbook describes a physiotherapist as having at least a baccalaureate qualification.

quick_facts.label: Entry-level education context
quick_facts.value: The National Commission for Allied and Healthcare Professions (NCAHP) physiotherapy handbook maps entry level to NSQF Level 6 after a minimum five-year Bachelor of Physiotherapy programme, including a mandatory foundation course in its recommended curriculum.

quick_facts.label: Clinical internship
quick_facts.value: The recommended BPT curriculum includes a 12-month rotatory clinical internship (minimum 2,016 hours) intended to prepare graduates to assess, diagnose and treat physiotherapy patients independently.

quick_facts.label: Typical employment settings
quick_facts.value: The handbook identifies employment across hospitals and nursing homes, sports teams, fitness centres, community rehabilitation and health-promotion services, in both public and private sectors, as well as independent clinics.

quick_facts.label: Postgraduate option
quick_facts.value: MPT is a route to advanced specialty practice, teaching and research. The handbook lists specialities including musculoskeletal, neuroscience, cardio-pulmonary, sports, paediatrics and neonatal, obstetrics and gynaecology, community, and oncological physiotherapy.

sections.heading: What a physiotherapist does
paragraphs: A physiotherapist assesses movement and function, interprets findings, identifies physiotherapy-related impairments and activity limitations, sets goals, and plans and evaluates interventions. Work includes safe delivery of therapeutic exercise, modalities and assistive-device or home-modification recommendations, with ongoing outcome measurement and adjustment of care.

The role also requires patient education, clinical documentation, consent-aware and ethical practice, and communication with patients and other members of the healthcare team. The precise duties, referral arrangements and permitted scope can vary by employer, facility and applicable state rules.
bullets: Conduct and record patient assessment; use standardised measures and clinical reasoning.
Plan, deliver and review rehabilitation interventions; monitor response and safety.
Teach exercises and self-management, and communicate with patients, families and interdisciplinary colleagues.
Maintain timely treatment records and follow clinical, ethical, safety and quality procedures.

sections.heading: Main route into the role
paragraphs: The standard academic route is BPT followed by clinical internship. In the NCAHP handbook’s recommended framework, entry level is mapped after a minimum five-year BPT programme and the bachelor’s curriculum includes a 12-month rotatory clinical internship. Applicants should check the current admission prospectus of each university or state counselling authority for its actual eligibility subjects, entrance process, duration, fees, internship arrangements and recognition requirements.

MPT is a postgraduate progression route rather than a prerequisite for initial physiotherapist practice. The handbook presents MPT as preparation for advanced specialty knowledge and skills, independent research, undergraduate teaching and specialty practice.
bullets: Choose a BPT programme and verify the awarding university, teaching hospital/clinical placement arrangements, and current eligibility directly with the institution.
Complete the prescribed academic and clinical requirements, including internship where required by the programme.
Consider MPT for specialist clinical development, teaching or research-oriented progression.

sections.heading: Skills to build
paragraphs: Core professional capability combines clinical assessment and treatment planning with safe intervention delivery. The national curriculum emphasises evidence-based decision-making, outcome evaluation, patient communication, accurate documentation, quality assurance and collaboration in multidisciplinary teams.

Practical interpersonal skills matter because rehabilitation commonly requires patients to understand and carry out exercises over time. The handbook specifically includes attentive listening, empathy, clarification, counselling for lifestyle change, written and verbal communication, and accurate record keeping.
bullets: Clinical reasoning: history-taking, examination, functional assessment, goal-setting and reassessment.
Treatment competence: therapeutic exercise, mobilisation and appropriate use of physiotherapy equipment/modalities.
Safety and professionalism: consent, risk awareness, infection-control and waste-management knowledge, ethical documentation and quality checks.
Communication and teamwork: explaining care, motivating patients, listening, documenting and coordinating with other professionals.
Evidence literacy: locating, critically appraising and applying research to practice.

sections.heading: Where physiotherapists work
paragraphs: Physiotherapists can work independently or as part of multidisciplinary care. The national handbook identifies hospitals, nursing homes, sports teams, fitness centres, community rehabilitation, health-promotion services and independent physiotherapy clinics in public and private sectors as employment settings.

Work setting shapes the caseload and facilities: hospital practice may include inpatient and outpatient rehabilitation, while sports-focused practice may require access to fitness measurement, exercise and movement-analysis resources. Government-hospital career tracks also exist: Safdarjung Hospital’s published recruitment-rules index includes posts for Physiotherapist, Senior Physiotherapist, Deputy Chief Physiotherapist, Chief Physiotherapist and Physiotherapist in its Sports Injury Centre.
bullets: Hospitals, nursing homes and rehabilitation departments.
Community rehabilitation and health-promotion services.
Sports teams, sports-injury services, fitness centres and gyms.
Independent or group physiotherapy clinics.
Government health institutions, subject to the qualifications and selection conditions in the relevant recruitment notice.

sections.heading: Progression and specialisation
paragraphs: Progression can combine clinical experience, greater responsibility, postgraduate study and leadership. The NCAHP handbook’s proposed job descriptions move from Level 6 activities such as treatment preparation, recording, equipment familiarity and supervised procedures to Level 7 professional-development and critical-evaluation capability, Level 8 consultation for urgent clinical response, Level 9 teaching and research involvement, and Level 10 protocol, quality-assurance and departmental-upgrade responsibilities. These are curriculum career-framework descriptions, not a universal employer pay or promotion rule.

MPT provides a recognised academic pathway for deeper specialty practice. Listed specialty areas include musculoskeletal, neuroscience, cardio-pulmonary, sports, paediatrics and neonatal, obstetrics and gynaecology, community, and oncological physiotherapy. MPT outcomes include specialty practice, research and undergraduate teaching capability. Senior and chief posts are employer-specific; candidates should use the current recruitment rules and vacancy notice rather than assuming a uniform experience threshold.
bullets: Build clinical breadth after BPT/internship, then develop a focused practice area.
Pursue MPT for advanced specialty, academic or research pathways.
Progress into senior clinical, supervisory, teaching, research, service-development or clinic-entrepreneurship roles depending on experience and employer requirements.
For government roles, follow the specific recruitment rules, eligibility criteria and selection process published by the recruiting organisation.
